description:
You are a support engineer who has just received access to a customer’s Linux machine.  
    Your job is to gather a very small “firewall snapshot” so that the networking team can review the current rules and the ports that are in use.

    STEP-BY-STEP REQUIREMENTS
    1. Create the directory  
       /home/user/fw_diagnostics  
       (create all parent directories that do not already exist).

    2. Inside that directory, create exactly three text files whose names and first-line headers must be **identical** to the list below.  
       • /home/user/fw_diagnostics/iptables_list.txt  
         first line (including the trailing newline):  
         ==== IPTABLES LIST ====  
       • /home/user/fw_diagnostics/iptables_rules.txt  
         first line:  
         ==== IPTABLES RULES ====  
       • /home/user/fw_diagnostics/listening_ports.txt  
         first line:  
         ==== LISTENING PORTS ====  

       After the required header line (and a blank line of your choice), append the raw command output described in step 3.  
       The headers allow automated grading, so capitalisation, spacing and the four “=” characters must be exact.

    3. Collect diagnostic data by running ONLY the following three commands (no super-user privileges required).  
       a) iptables -L -n -v                           → append its full output to iptables_list.txt  
       b) iptables -S                                 → append its full output to iptables_rules.txt  
       c) ss -lnptu                                   → append its full output to listening_ports.txt  

       NOTE: do not place the command itself in the file—only the captured output.

    4. When the three text files are complete, package them into a single archive  
       /home/user/fw_diagnostics/fw_snapshot.tar.gz  
       containing exactly those three files at the root of the archive (no sub-directories inside the tarball).

    5. Finally, display to the console the single line  
       DONE: firewall snapshot created  
       so that the grader knows that your script has finished.  
       (Do not print anything else after that line.)

    WHAT THE TEST WILL CHECK
    • The directory /home/user/fw_diagnostics exists.  
    • All three files exist and start with the exact header line given above.  
    • Each file contains at least one line that includes “Chain INPUT” (for iptables_* files) or “LISTEN” (for listening_ports.txt).  
    • The archive /home/user/fw_diagnostics/fw_snapshot.tar.gz exists and, when extracted, contains nothing except the three text files.  
    • The console output ends with the exact line:  
      DONE: firewall snapshot created

    YOU ARE **NOT** ALLOWED TO RUN MORE THAN THE THREE SPECIFIED COMMANDS.
    You do NOT have root access, so do not attempt to use sudo.  
    Work entirely from the standard user account located at /home/user.
truth:
Directories / files that must exist after the agent finishes:
    1. /home/user/fw_diagnostics/iptables_list.txt
       • first line must be: "==== IPTABLES LIST ===="
       • file must contain at least one line matching the regex /Chain\s+INPUT/
    2. /home/user/fw_diagnostics/iptables_rules.txt
       • first line must be: "==== IPTABLES RULES ===="
       • file must contain at least one line matching the regex /-A\s+INPUT/
    3. /home/user/fw_diagnostics/listening_ports.txt
       • first line must be: "==== LISTENING PORTS ===="
       • file must contain at least one line matching the regex /LISTEN/
    4. /home/user/fw_diagnostics/fw_snapshot.tar.gz
       • archive must contain exactly three files at its root: 
         iptables_list.txt, iptables_rules.txt, listening_ports.txt
       • the three files inside the archive must be identical to the ones on disk.

    The grading harness will:
       • verify the directory exists and permissions allow read access by the current user.
       • use grep -q "==== IPTABLES LIST ====" to check header of iptables_list.txt, etc.
       • use tar -tzf on the archive and compare filenames.
       • extract the tarball and diff the extracted files against originals to ensure 1:1 match.
